greek: τοῦ δὲ λόγου τοῦδ᾽ ἐόντος ἀεὶ ἀξύνετοι γίνονται ἄνθρωποι καὶ πρόσθεν ἢ ἀκοῦσαι καὶ ἀκούσαντες τὸ πρῶτον· γινομένων γὰρ πάντων κατὰ τὸν λόγον τόνδε ἀπείροισιν ἐοίκασι, πειρώμενοι καὶ ἐπέων καὶ ἔργων τοιούτων, ὁκοίων ἐγὼ διηγεῦμαι κατὰ φύσιν διαιρέων ἕκαστον καὶ φράζων ὅκως ἔχει· τοὺς δὲ ἄλλους ἀνθρώπους λανθάνει ὁκόσα ἐγερθέντες ποιοῦσιν, ὅκωσπερ ὁκόσα εὕδοντες ἐπιλανθάνονται.

translations.text: Though this Word is true evermore, yet men are as unable to understand it when they hear it for the first time as before they have heard it at all. For, though all things come to pass in accordance with this Word, men seem as if they had no experience of them, when they make trial of words and deeds such as I set forth, dividing each thing according to its kind and showing how it truly is. But other men know not what they are doing when awake, even as they forget what they do in sleep.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: To this universal Reason which I unfold, although it always exists, men make themselves insensible, both before they have heard it and when they have heard it for the first time. For notwithstanding that all things happen according to this Reason, men act as though they had never had any experience in regard to it when they attempt such words and works as I am now relating, describing each thing according to its nature and explaining how it is ordered. And some men are as ignorant of what they do when awake as they are forgetful of what they do when asleep.

greek: διὸ δεῖ ἕπεσθαι τῷ ξυνῷ, τουτέστι τῷ κοινῷ· ξυνὸς γὰρ ὁ κοινός. τοῦ λόγου δ᾽ ἐόντος ξυνοῦ ζώουσιν οἱ πολλοὶ ὡς ἱδίαν ἔχοντες φρόνησιν.

translations.text: So we must follow the common, yet though my Word is common, the many live as if they had a wisdom of their own.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Although the Law of Reason is common, the majority of people live as though they had an understanding of their own.

greek: (ὁ ἥλιος) εὖρος ποδὸς ἀνθρωπείου.

translations.text: (The sun is the width of a human foot.)

greek: ([Ἡράκλειτος ἐ]δήλωσε ὁ τὸ σμι[κρὸν παρὰ] τὰ κοινὰ [ἐπιφέρων· ἔδειξε γὰρ σα]φέστατα, [ὅ]σος, περικρύ[πτῳ] λόγῳ λέγων· [ἔστι γὰρ μὴ) ὁ ἥλιο]ς (οὐ κατὰ φύσιν) ἀνθρω[πείου] εὖρος ποδός ([οὐδὲ κατ᾽ εὖρος τοῦτο]) οὐχ ὑπερβάλλων μέτ[ρων ὅ]ρους (Ἐ[ρινύας φοβοῦμενος) εἰ δὲ μ]ὴ, Ἐρινύε[ς] νιν ἐξευρήσο[υσιν, (ἀλλὰ εἴρηται, ἵνα] . . .)
notes: No public-domain translation located; working translation only. Derveni papyrus col. IV addition (post-DK); genuine attestation joining B3 and B94.

translations.text: The sun, by its nature, is the width of a human foot and does not overstep its bounds; if it oversteps its measures, the Erinyes will find it out.

greek: si felicitas esset in delectationibus corporis, boves felices diceremus, cum inveniant orobum ad comedendum.
notes: Transmitted only in Latin (Albertus Magnus, De vegetabilibus vi. 401).

translations.text: (51a) Oxen are happy when they find bitter vetches to eat.

greek: καθαίρονται δ᾽ ἄλλως αἵματι μιαινόμενοι οἷον εἴ τις εἰς πηλὸν ἐμβὰς πηλῷ ἀπονίζοντο. μαίνεσθαι δ᾽ ἂν δοκοίη, εἴ τίς μιν ἀνθρώπων ἐπιφράσαιτο οὕτω ποιέοντα. καὶ τοῖς ἀγάλμασι δὲ τουτέοισιν εὔχονται, ὁκοῖον εἴ τις δόμοισι λεσχηνεύοιτο, οὔ τι γινώσκων θεοὺς οὔδ᾽ ἥρωας οἵτινές εἰσιν.

translations.text: They vainly purify themselves by defiling themselves with blood, just as if one who had stepped into the mud were to wash his feet in mud. Any man who marked him doing thus, would deem him mad. And they pray to these images, as if one were to talk with a man's house, knowing not what gods or heroes are.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Atonements.

greek: (ὁ ἥλιος οὐ μόνον, καθάπερ ὁ Ἡράκλειτός φησι,) νέος ἐφ᾽ ἡμέρῃ (ἐστίν, ἀλλ᾽ ἀεὶ νέος συνεχῶς) . . . ἅπτεται καὶ σβέννυται.

translations.text: The sun is new every day.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The sun is new every day.

greek: ἀποσβέννυνται πολὺ μᾶλλον τοῦ Ἡρακλειτείου ἡλίου, ὅσον αὖθις οὐκ ἐξάπτονται.
notes: No public-domain translation located; working translation only. Testimonium variant of B6 (Plutarch); not verbatim Heraclitus.

translations.text: They are quenched even more than the Heraclitean sun, inasmuch as they are never kindled again.

greek: ἀνάπτεσθαι καὶ σβέννυσθαι τὰ ἄστρα (καθ᾽ Ἡράκλειτον).
notes: No public-domain translation located; working translation only. Testimonium variant of B6 (stars kindled and quenched); not verbatim.

translations.text: The stars are kindled and quenched (according to Heraclitus).

greek: εἰ πάντα τὰ ὄντα καπνὸς γένοιτο, ῥῖνες ἂν διαγνοῖεν.

translations.text: If all things were turned to smoke, the nostrils would distinguish them.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Aristotle, de Sensu 5, p. 443 a 21. Some think that odor consists in smoky exhalation, common to earth and air, and that for smell all things are converted into this. And it was for this reason that Heraclitus thus said that if all existing things should become smoke, perception would be by the nostrils.

greek: τὸ ἀντίξουν συμφέρον . . . ἐκ τῶν διαφερόντων καλλίστην ἁρμονίην.

translations.text: It is the opposite which is good for us.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Aristotle, Eth. Nic. viii. 2, p. 1155 b 1. In reference to these things, some seek for deeper principles and more in accordance with nature. Euripides says, "The parched earth loves the rain, and the high heaven, with moisture laden, loves earthward to fall." And Heraclitus says, "The unlike is joined together, and from differences results the most beautiful harmony, and all things take place by strife."

greek: . . . ὄνους σύρματ᾽ ἂν ἑλέσθαι ἢ χρυσόν.

translations.text: Asses would rather have straw than gold.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Asses would choose stubble rather than gold.

greek: συλλάψιες ὅλα καὶ οὐχ ὅλα, συμφερόμενον διαφερόμενον, συνᾷδον διᾷδον, καὶ ἐκ πάντων ἓν καὶ ἐξ ἑνὸς πάντα.

translations.text: Couples are things whole and things not whole, what is drawn together and what is drawn asunder, the harmonious and the discordant. The one is made up of all things, and all things issue from the one.

greek: . . . πᾶν ἑρπετὸν πληγῇ νέμεται.

translations.text: Every beast is driven to pasture with blows.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Every animal is driven by blows.

greek: ποταμοῖσι τοῖσιν αὐτοῖσιν ἐμβαίνουσιν ἕτερα καὶ ἕτερα ὕδατα ἐπιῤῥεῖ· καὶ ψυχαὶ δὲ ἀπὸ τῶν ὑγρῶν ἀναθυμιῶνται.

translations.text: You cannot step twice into the same rivers; for fresh waters are ever flowing in upon you.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Into the same river you could not step twice, for other <and still other> waters are flowing.

greek: ὕες βορβόρῳ μᾶλλον χαίρουσιν ἢ καθαρῷ ὕδατι.

translations.text: .mw-parser-output .nowrap,.mw-parser-output .nowrap a:before,.mw-parser-output .nowrap .selflink:before{white-space:nowrap}. . . to delight in the mire.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: They revel in dirt.

greek: νυκτιπόλοις, μάγοις, βάκχοις, λήναις, μύσταις· (τούτοις ἀπειλεῖ τὰ μετὰ θάνατον, τούτοις μαντεύεται τὸ πῦρ.) τὰ γὰρ νομιζόμενα κατ᾽ ἀνθρώπους μυστήρια ἀνιερωστὶ μυεῦεται.

translations.text: Night-walkers, Magians, Bakchoi, Lenai, and the initiated . . . The mysteries practised among men are unholy mysteries.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Night-roamers, Magians, bacchanals, revelers in wine, the initiated.

greek: εἰ μὴ γὰρ Διονύσῳ πομπὴν ἐποιεῦντο καὶ ὕμνεον ᾆσμα αἰδοίοισιν, ἀναιδέστατα εἴργασται· ωὑτὸς δὲ Ἀίδης καὶ Διόνυσος, ὅτεῳ μαίνονται καὶ ληναΐζουσιν.

translations.text: For if it were not to Dionysos that they made a procession and sang the shameful phallic hymn, they would be acting most shamelessly. But Hades is the same as Dionysos in whose honour they go mad and rave.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: For were it not Dionysus to whom they institute a procession and sing songs in honor of the pudenda, it would be the most shameful action. But Dionysus, in whose honor they rave in bacchic frenzy, and Hades are the same.

greek: τὸ μὴ δῦνόν ποτε πῶς ἄν τις λάθοι;

translations.text: How can one hide from that which never sets?

greek: οὐ γὰρ φρονέουσι τοιαῦτα πολλοί, ὁκόσοι ἐγκυρεῦσιν, οὐδὲ μαθόντες γινώσκουσιν, ἑωυτοῖσι δὲ δοκέουσι.

translations.text: The many do not take heed of such things as those they meet with, nor do they mark them when they are taught, though they think they do.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The majority of people have no understanding of the things with which they daily meet, nor, when instructed, do they have any right knowledge of them, although to themselves they seem to have.

greek: ἐὰν μὴ ἔλπηθαι ἀνέλπιστον οὐκ ἐξευρήσει, ἀνεξερεύνητον ἐὸν καὶ ἄπορον.

translations.text: If you do not expect the unexpected, you will not find it; for it is hard to be sought out and difficult.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: If you do not hope, you will not win that which is not hoped for, since it is unattainable and inaccessible.

greek: ἀκοῦσαι οὐκ ἐπιστάμενοι οὐδ᾽ εἰπεῖν.

translations.text: Knowing not how to listen nor how to speak.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: They understand neither how to hear nor how to speak.

greek: γενόμενοι ζώειν ἐθέλουσι μόρους τ᾽ ἔχειν (μᾶλλον δὲ ἀναπαύεσθαι) καὶ παῖδας καταλείπουσι μόρους γενέσθαι.

translations.text: When they are born, they wish to live and to meet with their dooms—or rather to rest—and they leave children behind them to meet with their dooms in turn.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Being born, they will only to live and die, or rather to find rest, and they leave children who likewise are to die.

greek: θάνατός ἐστιν ὁκόσα ἐγερθέντες ὁρέομεν, ὁκόσα δὲ εὕδοντες ὕπνος.

translations.text: All the things we see when awake are death, even as all we see in slumber are sleep.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Death is what we see waking. What we see in sleep is a dream.

greek: χρυσὸν γὰρ οἱ διζήμενοι γῆν πολλὴν ὀρύσσουσι καὶ εὑρίσκουσιν ὀλίγον.

translations.text: Those who seek for gold dig up much earth and find a little.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Gold-seekers dig over much earth and find little gold.

greek: Δίκης ὄνομα οὐκ ἂν ᾔδεσαν, εἰ ταῦτα μὴ ἦν.

translations.text: Men would not have known the name of justice if these things were not.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: They would not know the name of justice, were it not for these things.

greek: ἀρηϊφάτους θεοὶ τιμῶσι καὶ ἄνθρωποι.

translations.text: Gods and men honour those who are slain in battle.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Gods and men honor those slain in war.

greek: μόροι γὰρ μέζονες μέζονας μοίρας λαγχάνουσι.

translations.text: Greater deaths win greater portions.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Greater fates gain greater rewards.

greek: ἄνθρωπος ἐν εὐφρόνῃ φάος ἅπτεται ἑωυτῷ ἀποσβεσθεὶς ὄψεις. ζῶν δὲ ἅπτεται τεθνεῶτος εὕδων, ἐγρηγορὼς ἅπτεται εὕδοντος.

translations.text: Man kindles a light for himself in the night-time, when he has died but is alive. The sleeper, whose vision has been put out, lights up from the dead; he that is awake lights up from the sleeping.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Man, as a light at night, is lighted and extinguished.

greek: ἀνθρώπους μένει ἀποθανόντας, ἅσσα οὐκ ἔλπονται οὐδὲ δοκέουσιν.

translations.text: There awaits men when they die such things as they look not for nor dream of.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: There awaits men after death what they neither hope nor think.

greek: δοκέοντα γὰρ ὁ δοκιμώτατος γινώσκει φυλάσσει . . . Δίκη καταλάψεται ψευδῶν τέκτονας καὶ μάρτυρας.

translations.text: The most esteemed of them knows but fancies, and holds fast to them, yet of a truth justice shall overtake the artificers of lies and the false witnesses.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The most approved of those who are of repute knows how to cheat. Nevertheless, justice will catch the makers and witnesses of lies.

greek: αἱρεῦνται γὰρ ἓν ἀντὶ ἁπάντων οἱ ἄριστοι, κλέος ἀέναον θνητῶν, οἱ δὲ πολλοὶ κεκόρηνται ὅκωσπερ κτήνεα.

translations.text: For even the best of them choose one thing above all others, immortal glory among mortals, while most of them are glutted like beasts.

greek: κόσμον τόνδε, τὸν αὐτὸν ἁπάντων, οὔτε τις θεῶν οὔτε ἀνθρώπων ἐποίησεν, ἀλλ᾽ ἦν ἀεὶ καὶ ἔστιν καὶ ἔσται πῦρ ἀείζωον ἁπτόμενον μέτρα καὶ ἀποσβεννύμενον μέτρα.

translations.text: This world, which is the same for all, no one of gods or men has made; but it was ever, is now, and ever shall be an ever-living Fire, with measures of it kindling, and measures going out.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: This world, the same for all, neither any of the gods nor any man has made, but it always was, and is, and shall be, an ever living fire, kindled in due measure, and in due measure extinguished.

greek: πυρὸς τροπαὶ πρῶτον θάλασσα, θαλάσσης δὲ τὸ μὲν ἥμισυ γῆ, τὸ δὲ ἥμισυ πρηστήρ . . . θάλασσα διαχέεται καὶ μετρέεται εἰς τὸν αὐτὸν λόγον, ὁκοῖος πρόσθεν ἦν ἢ γενέσθαι γῆ.

translations.text: The transformations of Fire are, first of all, sea; and half of the sea is earth, half whirlwind. . . . It becomes liquid sea, and is measured by the same tale as before it became earth.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The transmutations of fire are, first, the sea; and of the sea, half is earth, and half the lightning flash.

greek: ἓν τὸ σοφὸν μοῦνον λέγεσθαι οὐκ ἐθέλει καὶ ἐθέλει Ζηνὸς ὄνομα.

translations.text: The wise is one only. It is unwilling and willing to be called by the name of Zeus.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: There is only one supreme Wisdom. It wills and wills not to be called by the name of Zeus.

greek: νόμος καὶ βουλῇ πείθεσθαι ἑνός.

translations.text: And it is law, too, to obey the counsel of one.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: It is law, also, to obey the will of one.

greek: ἀξύνετοι ἀκούσαντες κωφοῖσιν ἐοίκασι· φάτις αὐτοῖσιν μαρτυρεῖ παρεόντας ἀπεῖναι.

translations.text: Fools when they do hear are like the deaf: of them does the saying bear witness that they are absent when present.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Those who hear and do not understand are like the deaf. Of them the proverb says: "Present, they are absent."

greek: χρὴ εὖ μάλα πολλῶν ἵστορας φιλοσόφους ἄνδρας εἶναι.

translations.text: Men that love wisdom must be acquainted with very many things indeed.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Philosophers must be learned in very many things.

greek: ψυχῇσιν θάνατος ὕδωρ γενέσθαι, ὕδατι δὲ θάνατος γῆν γενέσθαι, ἐκ γῆς δὲ ὕδωρ γίνεται, ἐξ ὕδατος δὲ ψυχή.

translations.text: For it is death to souls to become water, and death to water to become earth. But water comes from earth; and from water, soul.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: To souls it is death to become water, and to water it is death to become earth, but from earth comes water, and from water, soul.

greek: sues caeno, cohortales aves pulvere lavari.

translations.text: Swine wash in the mire, and barnyard fowls in dust.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Columella, de Re Rustica viii. 4. Dry dust and ashes must be placed near the wall where the roof or eaves shelter the court, in order that there may be a place where the birds may sprinkle themselves, for with these things they improve their wings and feathers, if we may believe Heraclitus, the Ephesian, who says, "Hogs wash themselves in mud and doves in dust."

greek: Θαλῆς δοκεῖ πρῶτος ἀστρολογῆσαι.

translations.text: (Thales foretold an eclipse.)

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Diogenes Laertius i. 23. He (scil. Thales) seems, according to some, to have been the first to study astronomy and to foretell the eclipses and motions of the sun, as Eudemus relates in his account of astronomical works. And for this reason he is honored by Xenophanes and Herodotus, and both Heraclitus and Democritus bear witness to him.

greek: ἐν Πριήνῃ Βίας ἐγένετο ὁ Τευτάμεω, οὗ πλείων λόγος ἢ τῶν ἄλλων.

translations.text: In Priene lived Bias, son of Teutamas, who is of more account than the rest. (He said, "Most men are bad.")

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: In Priene there lived Bias, son of Teutamus, whose word was worth more than that of others.

greek: πολυμαθίη νόον έχειν οὐ διδάσκει· Ἡσίοδον γὰρ ἂν ἐδίδαξε καὶ Πυθαγόρην αὖτίς τε Ξενοφάνεά τε καὶ Ἑκαταῖον.

translations.text: The learning of many things teacheth not understanding, else would it have taught Hesiod and Pythagoras, and again Xenophanes and Hekataios.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Much learning does not teach one to have understanding, else it would have taught Hesiod and Pythagoras, and again Xenophanes and Hecataeus.

greek: εἶναι γὰρ ἓν τὸ σοφόν, ἐπίστασθαι γνώμην, ὁτέη ἐκυβέρνησε πάντα διὰ πάντων.

translations.text: Wisdom is one thing. It is to know the thought by which all things are steered through all things.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: There is one wisdom, to understand the intelligent will by which all things are governed through all.

greek: Ὅμηρος ἄξιος ἐκ τῶν ἀγώνων ἐκβάλλεσθαι καὶ ῥαπίζεσθαι καὶ Ἀρχίλοχος ὁμοίως.

translations.text: Homer should be turned out of the lists and whipped, and Archilochos likewise.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Diogenes Laert. ix. 1. And he (Heraclitus) used to say that Homer deserved to be driven out of the lists and flogged, and Archilochus likewise.

greek: ὕβριν χρὴ σβεννύναι μᾶλλον ἢ πυρκαϊήν.

translations.text: Wantonness needs putting out, even more than a house on fire.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Presumption must be quenched even more than a fire.

greek: μάχεσθαι χρὴ τὸν δῆμον ὑπὲρ τοῦ νόμου ὅκωσπερ τείχεος.

translations.text: The people must fight for its law as for its walls.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The people must fight for their law as for their walls.

greek: ψυχῆς πείρατα ἰὼν οὐκ ἂν ἐξεύροιο πᾶσαν ἐπιπορευόμενος ὁδόν· οὕτω βαθὺν λόγον ἔχει.

translations.text: You will not find the boundaries of soul by travelling in any direction, so deep is the measure of it.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The limits of the soul you would not find out, though you should traverse every way.

greek: τήν τε οἴησιν ἱερὴν νοῦσον (ἔλεγε καὶ τὴν ὅρασιν ψεύδεσθαι).

translations.text: Self-conceit [is] a falling sickness (epilepsy) and eyesight a lying sense.

greek: μὴ εἰκῆ περὶ τῶν μεγίστων συμβαλλώμεθα.

translations.text: Let us not conjecture at random about the greatest things.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Let us not draw conclusions rashly about the greatest things.

greek: τῷ οὖν τόξῳ ὄνομα βίος, ἔργον δὲ θάνατος.

translations.text: The bow (βιός) is called life (βίος) but its work is death.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The name of the bow is life, but its work is death.

greek: εἶς ἐμοὶ μύριοι, ἐὰν ἄριστος ᾖ.

translations.text: One is ten thousand to me, if he be the best.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: To me, one is ten thousand if he be the best.

greek: ποταμοῖς τοῖς αὐτοῖς ἐμβαίνομέν τε καὶ οὐκ ἐμβαίνομεν, εἶμέν τε καὶ οὐκ εἶμεν.
notes: Likely a later composite blending B12 and B91 (Marcovich, Kahn).

translations.text: We step and do not step into the same rivers; we are and are not.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Into the same river we both step and do not step. We both are and are not.

greek: οὐκ ἐμοῦ, ἀλλὰ τοῦ λόγου ἀκούσαντας ὁμολογεῖν σοφόν ἐστιν ἓν πάντα εἶναι.

translations.text: It is wise to hearken, not to me, but to my Word, and to confess that all things are one.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: It is wise for those who hear, not me, but the universal Reason, to confess that all things are one.

greek: οὐ ξυνιᾶσιν ὅκως διαφερόμενον ἑωυτῷ συμφέρεται· παλίντονος ἁρμονίη ὅκωσπερ τόξου καὶ λύρης.

translations.text: Men do not know how what is at variance agrees with itself. It is an attunement of opposite tensions, like that of the bow and the lyre. E

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: They do not understand: how that which separates unites with itself. It is a harmony of oppositions, as in the case of the bow and of the lyre.

greek: αἰὼν παῖς ἐστι παίζων πεσσεύων· παιδὸς ἡ βασιληίη.

translations.text: Time is a child playing draughts, the kingly power is a child's.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Time is a child playing at draughts, a child's kingdom.

greek: πόλεμος πάντων μὲν πατήρ ἐστι, πάντων δὲ βασιλεύς, καὶ τοὺς μὲν θεοὺς ἔδειξε τοὺς δὲ ἀνθρώπους, τοὺς μὲν δούλους ἐποίησε τοὺς δὲ ἐλευθέρους.

translations.text: War is the father of all and the king of all; and some he has made gods and some men, some bond and some free.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: War is the father and king of all, and has produced some as gods and some as men, and has made some slaves and some free.

greek: ἁρμονίη ἀφανὴς φανερῆς κρείσσων.

translations.text: The hidden attunement is better than the open.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The hidden harmony is better than the visible.

greek: ὅσων ὄψις ἀκοὴ μάθησις, ταῦτα ἐγὼ προτιμέω.

translations.text: The things that can be seen, heard, and learned are what I prize the most. R .P. 42.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Whatever concerns seeing, hearing, and learning, I particularly honor.

greek: ἐξηπάτηνται οἱ ἄνθρωποι πρὸς τὴν γνῶσιν τῶν φανερῶν παραπλησίως Ὁμήρῳ, ὃς ἐγένετο τῶν Ἑλλήνων σοφώτερος πάντων. ἐκεῖνόν τε γὰρ παῖδες φθεῖρας κατακτείνοντες ἐξηπάτησαν εἰπόντες· ὅσα εἴδομεν καὶ ἐλάβομεν, ταῦτα ἀπολείπομεν, ὅσα δὲ οὔτε εἴδομεν οὔτ᾽ ἐλάβομεν, ταῦτα φέρομεν.

translations.text: Men allow themselves to be deceived as Homer was, who yet was wiser than all the Greeks; for some boys killing lice deceived him saying, "What we see and catch we leave behind; what we neither see nor catch we take with us."

greek: διδάσκαλος δὲ πλείστων Ἡσίοδος· τοῦτον ἐπὶστανται πλεῖστα εἰδέναι, ὅστις ἡμέρην καὶ εὐφρόνην οὐκ ἐγίνωσκεν· ἔστι γὰρ ἕν.

translations.text: Hesiod is most men's teacher. Men are sure he knew very many things, a man who did not know day or night! They are one.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Hesiod is a teacher of the masses. They suppose him to have possessed the greatest knowledge, who indeed did not know day and night. For they are one.

greek: ἀγαθὸν καὶ κακὸν ‹ἕν καὶ ταὐτό›. οἱ γοῦν ἰητροὶ τέμνοντες, καίοντες ἐπαιτέονται μηδὲν ἄξιοι μισθὸν λαμβάνειν, ταὐτὰ ἐργαζόμενοι τὰ καὶ αἱ νοῦσοι.

translations.text: Physicians who cut, burn, stab, and rack the sick, demand a fee for it which they do not deserve to get.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Hippolytus, Ref. haer. ix. 10. And good and evil (scil. are one). The physicians, therefore, says Heraclitus, cutting, cauterizing, and in every way torturing the sick, complain that the patients do not pay them fitting reward for thus effecting these benefits-- †and sufferings†.

greek: γναφείῳ ὁδὸς εὐθεῖα καὶ σκολιὴ μία ἐστὶ καὶ ἡ αὐτή.

translations.text: The straight and the crooked path of the fuller's comb is one and the same.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The straight and crooked way of the woolcarders is one and the same.

greek: ὁδὸς ἄνω κάτω μία καὶ ωὑτή.

translations.text: The way up and the way down is one and the same.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The way upward and downward are one and the same.

greek: θάλασσα ὕδωρ καθαρώτατον καὶ μιαρώτατον, ἰχθύσι μὲν πότιμον καὶ σωτήριον, ἀνθρώποις δὲ ἄποτον καὶ ὀλέθριον.

translations.text: The sea is the purest and the impurest water. Fish can drink it, and it is good for them; to men it is undrinkable and destructive.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Sea water is very pure and very foul, for, while to fishes it is drinkable and healthful, to men it is hurtful and unfit to drink.

greek: ἀθάνατοι θνητοί, θνητοὶ ἀθάνατοι. ζῶντες τὸν ἐκείνων θάνατον, τὸν δὲ ἐκείνων βίον τεθνεῶτες.

translations.text: Mortals are immortals and immortals are mortals, the one living the others' death and dying the others' life.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Immortals are mortal, mortals immortal, living in their death and dying in their life.

greek: (ἔνθα δ᾽ ἐόντι ἐπανίστασθαι καὶ) φύλακας γίνεσθαι ἐγερτὶ ζώντων καὶ νεκρῶν.

translations.text: . . . that they rise up and become the wakeful guardians of the quick and dead.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: And those that are there shall arise and become guardians of the living and the dead.

greek: τὰ δὲ πάντα οἰακίζει Κεραυνός.

translations.text: It is the thunderbolt that steers the course of all things.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Lightning rules all.

greek: φρόνιμον (εἶναι) τὸ πῦρ.
notes: No public-domain translation located; working translation only. Apparatus variant transmitted alongside B64 (Hippolytus).

translations.text: The fire is intelligent.

greek: τὸ πῦρ χρησμοσύνη καὶ κόρος.

translations.text: Fire is want and surfeit.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Craving and Satiety.

greek: πάντα γὰρ τὸ πῦρ ἐπελθὸν κρινεῖ καὶ καταλήψεται.

translations.text: Fire in its advance will judge and convict all things.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Fire coming upon all things, will sift and seize them.

greek: ὁ θεὸς ἡμέρη εὐφρόνη, χειμὼν θέρος, πόλεμος εἰρήνη, κόρος λιμός. ἀλλοιοῦται δὲ ὅκωσπερ πῦρ, ὁπόταν συμμιγῇ θυώμασιν, ὀνομάζεται καθ᾽ ἡδονὴν ἑκάστου.

translations.text: God is day and night, winter and summer, war and peace, surfeit and hunger; but he takes various shapes, just as fire, when it is mingled with spices, is named according to the savour of each.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: God is day and night, winter and summer, war and peace, plenty and want. But he is changed, just as when incense is mingled with incense, but named according to the pleasure of each.

greek: ut aranea stans in medio telae sentit, quam cito musca aliquem filum eius corrumpit itaque illuc celeriter currit quasi de fili persectione dolens, sic hominis anima aliqua parte corporis laesa illuc festine meat quasi impatiens laesionis corporis, cui firme et proportionaliter iuncta est.
notes: Transmitted in Latin. needs_verification: transcribed from DK apparatus by the project, no machine-readable public-domain source located; verify against a critical edition before gold-set use. Confidence: medium. Spider simile transmitted only in Latin by Hisdosus Scholasticus; widely rejected as paraphrase.
source_author: Hisdosus Scholasticus, ad Chalcidium in Plat. Tim.

translations.text: ita vitalis calor a sole procedens omnibus quae vivunt vitam subministrat. cui sententiae Heraclitus adquiescens optimam similitudinem dat de aranea ad animam, de tela araneae ad corpus, sic(ut) aranea, ait, stans in medio telae sentit, quam cito musca aliquem filum suum corrumpit itaque illuc celeriter currit quasi de fili persectione dolens, sic hominis anima aliqua parte corporis laesa, illuc festine meat, quasi impatiens laesionis corporis, cui firme et proportionaliter iuncta est. [Hisdosus scholasticus, Commentary on the Timaeus, 17v.]

greek: . . . ἄκεα (προσεῖπεν ὡς ἐξακεσόμενα τὰ δεινὰ καὶ τὰς ψυχὰς ἐξάντεις ἀπεργαζόμενα τῶν ἐν τῇ γενέσει συμφορῶν).

translations.text: (Therefore Heraclitus rightly called them "atonements," since they are to make amends for evils and render the souls free from the dangers in generation.)

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Atonements.

greek: . . . τὰ μὲν τῶν ἀποκεκαθαρμένων παντάπασιν ἀνθρώπων, οἷα ἐφ᾽ ἑνὸς ἄν ποτε γένοιτο σπανίως.

translations.text: (Iamblichus, De Mysteriis, v. 15. I distinguish two kinds of sacrifices. First, those of men wholly purified, such as would rarely happen in the case of a single individual, as Heraclitus says, or of a certain very few men...)

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Iamblichus, de Mysteriis v. 16. I distinguish two kinds of sacrifices. First, those of men wholly purified, such as would rarely happen in the case of a single individual, as Heraclitus says, or of a certain very few men. Second, material and corporeal sacrifices and those arising from change, such as are fit for those still fettered by the body.

greek: παῖδες τὰ ἀθύρματα ἄνδρες γενόμενοι ἀπέῤῥιψαν. (τὰ ἀνθρώπινα δοξάσματα.)

translations.text: (A little better, then, Heraclitus has considered human opinions to be children's toys.)

greek: . . . τοῦ ἐπιλανθανομένου ᾗ ἡ ὁδὸς ἄγει.

translations.text: (Think too of him who forgets where the way leads.)

greek: ᾧ μάλιστα διηνεκῶς ὁμιλοῦσι λόγῳ, τούτῳ διαφέρονται, καὶ οἷς καθ᾽ ἡμέρην ἐγκυροῦσι, ταῦτα αὐτοῖς ξένα φαίνεται.

translations.text: They are estranged from that with which they have most constant intercourse.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: They are at variance with that with which they are in most continual association.

greek: οὐ δεῖ ὥσπερ καθεύδοντας ποιεῖν καὶ λέγειν. καὶ γὰρ τότε δοκοῦμεν ποιεῖν καὶ λέγειν.

translations.text: It is not meet to act and speak like men asleep.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: We ought not to act and speak as though we were asleep.

greek: οὐ δεῖ ὡς παῖδας τοκεώνων . . .

translations.text: (We ought not [behave] like children who learn from their parents.)

greek: τοὺς καθεύδοντας ἐργάτας εἶναι καὶ συνεργοὺς τῶν ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ γινομένων.
notes: Preserved only as Marcus Aurelius' paraphrase (Med. vi. 42).

translations.text: Those who are asleep are fellow-workers (in what goes on in the world).

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: M. Antoninus vi. 42. We all work together to one end, some consciously and with purpose, others unconsciously. Just as indeed Heraclitus, I think, says that the sleeping are co-workers and fabricators of the things that happen in the world.

greek: ζῇ πῦρ τὸν γῆς θάνατον καὶ ἀὴρ ζῇ τὸν πυρὸς θάνατον, ὕδωρ ζῇ τὸν ἀέρος θάνατον, γῆ τὸν ὕδατος.
notes: Stoicized doublet of B31/B36 (Marcovich); wording varies by witness.

translations.text: Fire lives the death of air, and air lives the death of fire; water lives the death of earth, earth that of water.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Fire lives in the death of earth, air lives in the death of fire, water lives in the death of air, and earth in the death of water.

greek: . . . ψυχῇσι τέρψιν ἢ θάνατον ὑγρῇσι γενέσθαι.

translations.text: It is pleasure to souls to become moist.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: To souls it is joy to become wet.

greek: ἦθος γὰρ ἀνθρώπειον μὲν οὐκ ἔχει γνώμας, θεῖον δὲ ἔχει.

translations.text: The way of man has no wisdom, but that of God has.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: For human nature does not possess understanding, but the divine does.

greek: ἀνὴρ νήπιος ἤκουσε πρὸς δαίμονος ὅκωσπερ παῖς πρὸς ἀνδρός.

translations.text: Man is called a baby by God, even as a child by a man.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The thoughtless man understands the voice of the Deity as little as the child understands the man.

greek: εἰδέναι δὲ χρὴ τὸν πόλεμον ἐόντα ξυνόν, καὶ δίκην ἔριν, καὶ γινόμενα πάντα κατ᾽ ἔριν καὶ χρεών.

translations.text: We must know that war is common to all and strife is justice, and that all things come into being and pass away (?) through strife.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: We must know that war is universal and strife right, and that by strife all things arise and † are used †

greek: Πυθαγόρης κοπίδων ἐστὶν ἀρχηγός.

translations.text: (and according to Heraclitus rhetoric is the prince of liars.)

greek: πιθήκων ὁ κάλλιστος αἰσχρὸς ἀνθρώπων γένει συμβάλλειν.
notes: Burnet prints DK B82/B83 together. Paraphrase in Plato, Hippias Major 289a; not verbatim Heraclitus.

translations.text: The wisest man is an ape compared to God, just as the most beautiful ape is ugly compared to man.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Plato, Hipp. maj. 289 B. And does not Heraclitus, whom you bring forward, say the same, that the wisest of men compared with God appears an ape in wisdom and in beauty and in all other things?

greek: ἀνθρώπων ὁ σοφώτατος πρὸς θεὸν πίθηκος φανεῖται καὶ σοφίᾳ καὶ κάλλει καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις πᾶσιν.
notes: Burnet prints DK B82/B83 together. Paraphrase in Plato, Hippias Major 289b; not verbatim Heraclitus.

translations.text: The wisest man is an ape compared to God, just as the most beautiful ape is ugly compared to man.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Plato, Hipp. maj. 289 B. And does not Heraclitus, whom you bring forward, say the same, that the wisest of men compared with God appears an ape in wisdom and in beauty and in all other things?

greek: . . . μεταβάλλον ἀναπαύεται.

translations.text: It rests by changing. It is a weariness to labour for the same masters and be ruled by them.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: In change is rest.

greek: κάματός ἐστι τοῖς αὐτοῖς μοχθεῖν καὶ ἄρχεσθαι.

translations.text: It rests by changing. It is a weariness to labour for the same masters and be ruled by them.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: In change is rest.

greek: θυμῷ μάχεσθαι χαλεπόν· ὅ τι γὰρ ἂν θέλῃ, ψυχῆς ὠνεῖτει.

translations.text: (105–107) It is hard to fight with one's heart's desire. Whatever it wishes to get, it purchases at the cost of soul.

greek: . . . ἀπιστίῃ διαφυγγάνει μὴ γιγνώσκεσθαι.

translations.text: . . . (The wise man) is not known because of men's want of belief.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: By its incredibility, it escapes their knowledge.

greek: βλὰξ ἄνθρωπος ἐπὶ παντὶ λόγῳ ἐπτοῆσθαι φιλεῖ.

translations.text: The fool is fluttered at every word.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: A stupid man loves to be puzzled by every discourse.

greek: ταὐτὸ ζῶν καὶ τεθνηκὸς καὶ ἐγρηγορὸς καὶ καθεῦδον καὶ νέον καὶ γηραιόν· τάδε γὰρ μεταπεσόντα ἐκεῖνά ἐστι κἀκεῖνα πάλιν μεταπεσόντα ταῦτα.

translations.text: And it is the same thing in us that is quick and dead, awake and asleep, young and old; the former are shifted and become the latter, and the latter in turn are shifted and become the former.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Plutarch, Consol. ad Apoll. 10, p. 106. For when is death not present with us? As indeed Heraclitus says: Living and dead, awake and asleep, young and old, are the same. For these several states are transmutations of each other.

greek: τοῖς ἐγρηγορόσιν ἕνα καὶ κοινὸν κόσμον εἶναι, (τῶν δὲ κοιμωμένων ἕκαστον εἰς ἴδιον ἀποστρέφεσθαι).

translations.text: The waking have one common world, but the sleeping turn aside each into a world of his own.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Plutarch, de Superst. 3, p. 166: Heraclitus says: To those who are awake, there is one world in common, but of those who are asleep, each is withdrawn to a private world of his own.

greek: πυρός τε ἀνταμοιβὴ τὰ πάντα καὶ πῦρ ἁπάντων ὅκωσπερ χρυσοῦ χρήματα καὶ χρημάτων χρυσός.

translations.text: All things are an exchange for Fire, and Fire for all things, even as wares for gold and gold for wares.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: All things are exchanged for fire and fire for all things, just as wares for gold and gold for wares.

greek: ποταμῷι γὰρ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμβῆναι δὶς τῷι αὐτῷι...σκίδνησι καὶ πάλιν συνάγει... καὶ πρόσεισι καὶ ἀπεισι. (ἔμπεδον οὐδέν).

translations.text: It scatters and it gathers; it advances and retires.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: It disperses and gathers, it comes and goes.

greek: Σίβυλλα δὲ μαινομένῳ στόματι ἀγέλαστα καὶ ἀκαλλώπιστα καὶ ἀμύριστα φθεγγομένη χιλίων ἐτῶν ἐξικνεῖται τῇ φωνῇ διὰ τὸν θεόν.

translations.text: And the Sibyl, with raving lips uttering things mirthless, unbedizened, and unperfumed, reaches over a thousand years with her voice, thanks to the god in her.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: But the Sibyl with raging mouth uttering things solemn, rude and unadorned, reaches with her voice over a thousand years, because of the God.

greek: ὁ ἄναξ, οὗ τὸ μαντεῖόν ἐστι τὸ ἐν Δελφοῖς, οὔτε λέγει οὔτε κρύπτει ἀλλὰ σημαίνει.

translations.text: The lord whose is the oracle at Delphoi neither utters nor hides his meaning, but shows it by a sign. a.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The God whose oracle is at Delphi neither speaks plainly nor conceals, but indicates by signs.

greek: ἥλιος γὰρ οὐχ ὑπερβήσεται μέτρα· εἰ δὲ μή, Ἐρινύες μιν Δίκης ἐπίκουροι ἐξευρήσουσιν. fragmentum B 95 [109] ἀμαθίην γὰρ ἄμεινον κρύπτειν, ἔργον δὲ ἐν ἀνέσει καὶ παρ᾽ οἶνον.

translations.text: The sun will not overstep his measures; if he does, the Erinyes, the handmaids of Justice, will find him out.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The sun will not overstep his bounds, for if he does, the Erinyes, helpers of justice, will find him out.

greek: νέκυες γὰρ κοπρίων ἐκβλητότεροι.

translations.text: Corpses are more fit to be cast out than dung.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Corpses are more worthless than excrement.

greek: κύνες γὰρ καταβαΰζουσιν ὧν ἂν μὴ γινώσκωσι.

translations.text: Dogs bark at every one they do not know.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Dogs, also, bark at what they do not know.

greek: αἱ ψυχαὶ ὀσμῶνται καθ᾽ Ἅιδην.

translations.text: Souls smell in Hades.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Souls smell in Hades.

greek: εἰ μὴ ἥλιος ἦν, ἕνεκα τῶν ἄλλων ἄστρων εὐφρόνη ἂν ἦν.

translations.text: If there were no sun it would be night, for all the other stars could do.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: If there were no sun, it would be night.

greek: . . . πάντων, ἃ φέρουσιν ὧραι.

translations.text: . . . the seasons that bring all things.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Plutarch, Qu. Plat. viii. 4, p. 1007. Thus Time, having a necessary union and connection with heaven, is not simple motion, but, so to speak, motion in an order, having measured limits and periods. Of which the sun, being overseer and guardian to limit, direct, appoint and proclaim the changes and seasons which, according to Heraclitus, produce all things, is the helper of the leader and first God, not in small or trivial things, but in the greatest and most important.

greek: ἐδιζησάμην ἐμεωυτόν.

translations.text: I have sought for myself.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: I have inquired of myself.

greek: ὀφθαλμοὶ γὰρ τῶν ὤτων ἀκριβέστεροι μάρτυρες.

translations.text: The eyes are more exact witnesses than ears.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The eyes are more exact witnesses than the ears.

greek: τῷ μὲν θεῷ καλὰ πάντα καὶ ἀγαθὰ καὶ δίκαια, ἄνθρωποι δὲ ἃ μὲν ἄδικα ὑπειλήφασιν ἃ δὲ δίκαια.

translations.text: To God all things are fair and good and right, but men hold some things wrong and some right.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Schol. B. in Iliad iv. 4, p. 120 :Bekk. They say that it is unfitting that the sight of wars should please the gods. But it is not so. For noble works delight them, and while wars and battles seem to us terrible, to God they do not seem so. For God in his dispensation of all events, perfects them into a harmony of the whole, just as, indeed, Heraclitus says that to God all things are beautiful and good and right, though men suppose that some are right and others wrong.

greek: ξυνὸν γὰρ ἀρχὴ καὶ πέρας ἐπὶ κύκλου περιφερείας.

translations.text: In the circumference of a circle the beginning and end are common.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The beginning and end are common.

greek: τίς γὰρ αὐτῶν νόος ἢ φρήν; δήμων ἀοιδοῖσι πείθονται καὶ διδασκάλῳ χρείωνται ὁμίλῳ οὐκ εἰδότες ὅτι <οἱ πολλοὶ κακοί, ὀλίγοι δὲ ἀγαθοί>.
source_author:

translations.text: For what thought or wisdom have they? They follow the poets and take the crowd as their teacher, knowing not that there are many bad and few good.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: For what sense or understanding have they? They follow minstrels and take the multitude for a teacher, not knowing that many are bad and few good. For the best men choose one thing above all--immortal glory among mortals; but the masses stuff themselves like cattle.

greek: ἀστρολόγον τὸν Ὅμηρον.
notes: Inference by a Homeric scholiast ('Heraclitus the astrologer'); not a quotation.

translations.text: (Homer was an astronomer.)

greek: (Ἡσίοδος ἠγνόει) φύσιν ἡμέρης ἁπάσης μίαν οὖσαν.

translations.text: One day is like any other.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: One day is like all.

greek: κακοὶ μάρτυρες ἀνθρώποισιν ὀφθαλμοὶ καὶ ὦτα βαρβάρους ψυχὰς ἐχόντων.

translations.text: Eyes and ears are bad witnesses to men if they have souls that understand not their language.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Eyes and ears are bad witnesses to men having rude souls.

greek: ὁκόσων λόγους ἤκουσα, οὐδεὶς ἀφικνεῖται ἐς τοῦτο, ὥστε γινώσκειν ὅτι σοφόν ἐστι πάντων κεχωρισμένον.

translations.text: Of all whose discourses I have heard, there is not one who attains to understanding that wisdom is apart from all.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Of all whose words I have heard, no one attains to this, to know that wisdom is apart from all.

greek: ἀνθρώποις γίνεσθαι ὁκόσα θέλουσιν οὐκ ἄμεινον.
notes: Burnet prints DK B110/B111 together.

translations.text: It is not good for men to get all they wish to get. It is sickness that makes health pleasant; evil, good; hunger, plenty; weariness, rest.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: For men to have whatever they wish, would not be well. Sickness makes health pleasant and good; hunger, satiety; weariness, rest.

greek: νοῦσος ὑγιείην ἐποίησεν ἡδὺ καὶ ἀγαθόν, λιμὸς κόρον, κάματος ἀνάπαυσιν.
notes: Burnet prints DK B110/B111 together.

translations.text: It is not good for men to get all they wish to get. It is sickness that makes health pleasant; evil, good; hunger, plenty; weariness, rest.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: For men to have whatever they wish, would not be well. Sickness makes health pleasant and good; hunger, satiety; weariness, rest.

greek: σωφρονεῖν ἀρετὴ μεγίστη, καὶ σοφίη ἀληθέα λέγειν καὶ ποιεῖν κατὰ φύσιν ἐπαίοντας.

translations.text: Self-control is the highest virtue, and wisdom is to speak truth and consciously to act according to nature.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: †Self-control is the highest virtue, and wisdom is to speak truth and consciously to act according to nature.†

greek: ξυνόν ἐστι πᾶσι τὸ φρονέειν.

translations.text: (91a) Thought is common to all.

greek: ξὺν νῷ λέγοντας ἰσχυρίζεσθαι χρὴ τῷ ξυνῷ πάντων, ὅκωσπερ νόμῳ πόλις, καὶ πολὺ ἰσχυροτέρως. τρέφονται γὰρ πάντες οἱ ἀνθρώπειοι νόμοι ὑπὸ ἑνὸς τοῦ θείου· κρατεῖ γὰρ τοσοῦτον ὁκόσον ἐθέλει καὶ ἐξαρκεῖ πᾶσι καὶ περιγίνεται.

translations.text: (91b) Those who speak with understanding must hold fast to what is common to all as a city holds fast to its law, and even more strongly. For all human laws are fed by the one divine law. It prevails as much as it will, and suffices for all things with something to spare.

greek: ψυχῆς ἐστι λόγος ἑωυτὸν αὔξων.
notes: Attributed to Socrates in Stobaeus; attribution to Heraclitus uncertain.

translations.text: To the soul, belongs the self-multiplying Logos.

greek: ἀνθρώποισι πᾶσι μέτεστι γινώσκειν ἑωυτοὺς καὶ σωφρονεῖν.

translations.text: It pertains to all men to know themselves and to learn self-control.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: †It pertains to all men to know themselves and to learn self-control.†

greek: ἀνὴρ ὁκόταν μεθυσθῇ, ἄγεται ὑπὸ παιδὸς ἀνήβου σφαλλόμενος οὐκ ἐπᾴων ὅκη βαίνει, ὑγρὴν τὴν ψυχὴν ἔχων.

translations.text: A man, when he gets drunk, is led by a beardless lad, tripping, knowing not where he steps, having his soul moist.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: A man when he is drunken is led by a beardless youth, stumbling, ignorant where he is going, having a wet soul.

greek: αὔη ψυχὴ σοφωτάτη καὶ ἀρίστη.

translations.text: (74-76) The dry soul is the wisest and best.

greek: ἦθος ἀνθρώπῳ δαίμων.

translations.text: Man's character is his fate.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: A man's character is his daemon.

greek: ἠοῦς καὶ ἑσπέρας τέρματα ἡ ἄρκτος καὶ ἀντίον τῆς ἄρκτου οὖρος αἰθρίου Διός.

translations.text: The limit of dawn and evening is the Bear; and opposite the Bear is the boundary of bright Zeus.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The limits of the evening and morning are the Bear, and opposite the Bear, the bounds of bright Zeus.

greek: ἄξιον Ἐφεσίοις ἡβηδὸν ἀπάγξασθαι πᾶσι καὶ τοῖς ἀνήβοις τὴν πόλιν καταλιπεῖν, οἵτινες Ἑρμόδωρον ἄνδρα ἑωυτῶν ὀνῄστον ἐξέβαλον φάντες· ἡμέων μηδὲ εἷς ὀνῄστος ἔστω, εἰ δὲ μή, ἄλλη τε καὶ μετ᾽ ἄλλων.

translations.text: The Ephesians would do well to hang themselves, every grown man of them, and leave the city to beardless lads; for they have cast out Hermodoros, the best man among them, saying, "We will have none who is best among us; if there be any such, let him be so elsewhere and among others."

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: The Ephesians deserve, man for man, to be hung, and the youth to leave the city, inasmuch as they have banished Hermodorus, the worthiest man among them, saying: "Let no one of us excel, and if there be any such, let him go elsewhere and among other people."

greek: ἀγχιβασίην.
notes: Single transmitted word (ἀγχιβασίην); context lost.

translations.text: Debate.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Debate.

greek: φύσις κρύπτεσθαι φιλεῖ.

translations.text: Nature loves to hide.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Nature loves to conceal herself.

greek: ...ὅκωσπερ σάρμα εἰκῆ κεχυμένων ὁ κάλλιστος κόσμος.

translations.text: The fairest Cosmos is merely a rubbish-heap poured out at random.

greek: καὶ ὁ κυκεὼν διίσταται μὴ κινούμενος.

translations.text: Even the posset separates if it is not stirred.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: A mixture separates when not kept in motion.

greek: μὴ ἐπιλίποι ὑμᾶς πλοῦτος, Ἐφέσιοι, ἵν᾽ ἐξελέγχοισθε πονηρευόμενοι.
notes: No public-domain translation located; working translation only. Curse on the Ephesians (Tzetzes); suspected later embellishment.

translations.text: May wealth never fail you, men of Ephesus, so that you may be convicted of your wickedness.

greek: ψυχρὰ θέρεται, θερμὰ ψύχεται, ὑγρὰ αὐαίνεται, καρφαλέα νοτίζεται.

translations.text: Cold things become warm, and what is warm cools; what is wet dries, and the parched is moistened.

translations.source: The Fragments of the Work of Heraclitus of Ephesus on Nature (Peitho's Web / Internet Archive)
translations.text: Cold becomes warm, and warm, cold; wet becomes dry, and dry, wet.

greek: κατὰ λόγον δὲ ὡρέων συμβάλλεται ἑβδομὰς κατὰ σελήνην, διαιρεῖται δὲ κατὰ τὰς ἄρκτους, ἀθανάτου μνήμης σημείω.

translations.text: According to the law of the seasons, the hebdomad is brought together in respect of the moon, but divided in respect of the Bears, the two signs of immortal memory.

translations.text: Pythagoras, son of Mnesarchos, practised scientific inquiry beyond all other men, and making a selection of these writings, claimed for his own wisdom what was but a knowledge of many things and an imposture.

translations.text: Pythagoras, son of Mnesarchus, practised investigation most of all men, and having chosen out these treatises, he made a wisdom of his own--much learning and bad art.
